target.name: Applied Atomics
target.founder: Ben Kellie, Paul Keutelian, Mark Lambert

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $12M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
Applied Atomics is a seed-stage nuclear energy company founded by Ben Kellie, Paul Keutelian, and Mark Lambert that has raised $12M to date. The nuclear energy sector includes 3 public comps (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private peers, though the private set is fragmented across multiple development stages and valuation tiers.

The stage-progression method is the applicable triangulation approach here. Using total capital raised ($12M) as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.5x multiplier typical of seed-to-next-stage progression yields $30M. The peer-median method was not applicable due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables within the nuclear energy universe; while 8 private peers exist in the sector, filtering for stage and valuation proximity yielded fewer than the three minimum datapoints required for reliable median analysis. Constructing a broader cross-sector median would introduce excessive noise given Applied Atomics' specialized technology and regulatory environment.

The single-method triangulation supports a valuation range of $26M to $34M, with a median estimate of $30M. Key uncertainties include the absence of closer private market precedents in nuclear energy at seed stage, limiting calibration against actual transaction multiples in this subsector. Sensitivity to the 2.5x step-up multiplier itself—which reflects broad stage norms but may not account for nuclear-specific capital intensity or regulatory risk—represents a material source of range width. A revised estimate should incorporate additional data as comparable seed or early-stage nuclear transactions emerge.
